title: The UK and International Institutions
overview: The United Kingdom plays an active part in international affairs. It works with other countries to promote peace, security, human rights, and economic progress. Through international organisations like the Commonwealth, United Nations, NATO, and Council of Europe, Britain helps tackle global challenges and supports cooperation.

study_sections.heading: Commonwealth
study_sections.content: Commonwealth: voluntary association of 56 independent countries, most former British Empire. British Monarch is ceremonial Head. Members work together to promote democracy, human rights, development. Examples: Canada, Australia, India, Nigeria, New Zealand, South Africa.

study_sections.heading: United Nations
study_sections.content: UN founded 1945 after Second World War to maintain peace and promote cooperation. UK is one of 5 permanent members of UN Security Council (with USA, France, China, Russia). UK contributes troops and aid to UN peacekeeping and humanitarian missions. HQ in New York.

study_sections.heading: NATO and Council of Europe
study_sections.content: NATO formed 1949 by Western allies for mutual defence. If one member attacked, others help defend. UK is founding member and leading contributor. Council of Europe founded 1949, separate from EU. Works to protect human rights, democracy, rule of law across Europe. European Court of Human Rights in Strasbourg enforces principles.

study_sections.heading: Other Organizations
study_sections.content: European Union: UK was member 1973-2020 (Brexit). Now maintains trade and security cooperation under new agreements. UK belongs to WTO, G7, G20 coordinating global trade and economic policy. Through these institutions, Britain promotes peace, free trade, shared values worldwide.

key_facts: The UK actively engages in international institutions to promote peace, democracy, human rights, and development.
The United Nations, founded in 1945, has the UK as a founding member and permanent Security Council member with veto powers.
The UK contributes troops, diplomats, and funding to UN peacekeeping, humanitarian work, and sustainable development goals.
The Commonwealth links over 50 independent countries with the British monarch as Head, promoting democracy, development, human rights, and education through the 2013 Charter.
Events like the Commonwealth Games build friendship and cultural exchange among members.
NATO, created in 1949, is a military alliance whose Article 5 guarantees collective defence; the UK is a founding member providing forces and strategic support.
The alliance now also tackles counterterrorism, cyber defence, and humanitarian missions.
The Council of Europe, also founded in 1949, promotes human rights, democracy, and the rule of law, with the UK helping to draft the European Convention on Human Rights.
The European Court of Human Rights in Strasbourg enforces the Convention, and the Human Rights Act 1998 brings those rights into UK law.
The G7 unites leading economies, including the UK, to discuss economic stability, security, and climate issues.
The World Trade Organization regulates global trade; the UK has been an independent member since leaving the EU in 2020 and supports free trade and dispute resolution.
The Organisation for Security and Co-operation in Europe promotes peace and democracy; the UK takes part in election monitoring and peacebuilding.
Working through these organisations helps the UK support global stability, international law, and shared progress.
memory_hook: See Britain’s diplomacy as a network of partnerships—UN, Commonwealth, NATO, rights courts, and trade forums woven together for shared security and progress.
